sentences.text: Der boede engang tre brødre på en gård.
sentences.translation: Once there lived three brothers on a farm.

words.grammar: bo (to live, reside) · boede (past) · har boet (perfect) · weak verb
en gård · gården (the farm) · gårde (plural) · gårdene (the farms)

sentences.text: De to ældste brødre var meget kloge, men den yngste hed Klods-Hans.
sentences.translation: The two oldest brothers were very clever, but the youngest was called Klods-Hans.

words.grammar: klog (common) · klogt (neuter) · kloge (pl./def.) · superlative: klogest/klogeste
hedde (to be called/named) · hed (past) · har heddet (perfect) · irregular verb

sentences.text: Kongen havde en datter, og hun ville gerne giftes med en klog mand.
sentences.translation: The king had a daughter, and she wanted to marry a clever man.

words.grammar: en konge · kongen (the king) · konger (plural)
en datter · datteren (the daughter) · døtre (plural)

sentences.text: De to kloge brødre red til slottet på flotte heste.
sentences.translation: The two clever brothers rode to the castle on fine horses.

words.grammar: ride (to ride) · red (past) · har redet (perfect) · strong verb
flot (common) · flot (neuter, irregular - no -t added) · flotte (pl./def.)

sentences.text: Klods-Hans ville også af sted, så han satte sig på en gedebuk.
sentences.translation: Klods-Hans also wanted to go, so he sat himself on a billy goat.

words.grammar: så (result conjunction) · coordinating, like "og"/"men" · normal word order continues after it
sætte sig (to sit down, reflexive) · satte (past) · har sat sig (perfect) · weak verb + reflexive pronoun

sentences.text: På vejen fandt han en død krage, en gammel træsko og noget mudder.
sentences.translation: On the way he found a dead crow, an old wooden shoe, and some mud.

words.grammar: en krage · kragen (the crow) · krager (plural)
mudder (uncountable, no plural) · mudderet (the mud)

sentences.text: Han puttede alting i lommen, for det kunne måske bruges.
sentences.translation: He put everything in his pocket, because it could perhaps be used.

words.grammar: for (causal, coordinating) · comma before it · followed by normal (not V2-inverted) word order
bruge (to use) · bruges (passive -s form, present) · can be used

sentences.text: Da brødrene kom til prinsessen, kunne de ikke svare på hendes spørgsmål.
sentences.translation: When the brothers arrived at the princess, they couldn't answer her questions.

words.grammar: da (subordinating, only for past-time "when") · contrast with når (present/future "when")
kunne (modal: can/could) · kunne (past) · kunne ikke = couldn't

sentences.text: Klods-Hans svarede med kragen, træskoen og mudderet, og prinsessen lo højt.
sentences.translation: Klods-Hans answered with the crow, the wooden shoe, and the mud, and the princess laughed loudly.

words.grammar: svare (to answer) · svarede (past) · har svaret (perfect) · weak verb
le (to laugh) · lo (past) · har let (perfect) · strong verb

sentences.text: Hun sagde: "Han er sjov, og jeg vil gifte mig med ham!"
sentences.translation: She said: "He is funny, and I want to marry him!"

words.grammar: sjov (common) · sjovt (neuter) · sjove (pl./def.)
gifte sig med (to marry someone, reflexive) · gifter/giftede/har giftet · always reflexive + med

sentences.text: Kongen blev overrasket, men han holdt sit ord.
sentences.translation: The king was surprised, but he kept his word.

words.grammar: overrasket (common/neuter, same form) · past participle used as adjective · blev overrasket = was surprised
holde (to hold/keep) · holdt (past) · har holdt (perfect) · holde sit ord = to keep one's word

sentences.text: Klods-Hans og prinsessen blev gift, og han blev konge.
sentences.translation: Klods-Hans and the princess got married, and he became king.

words.grammar: en konge · kongen (the king) · konger (plural)

metadata.adaptedFrom: Adapted from H.C. Andersen (1855), simplified for A2 learners

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Han ville også af sted, så han satte sig på en gedebuk.
grammarPoints.explanation: Så links a cause to its result. Like other coordinating conjunctions (og, men, for), it doesn't change the word order of the clause that follows it.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Han puttede alting i lommen, for det kunne måske bruges.
grammarPoints.explanation: Both mean "because", but for keeps normal word order after the comma, while fordi (a subordinating conjunction) would push the verb to the end of its clause.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Da brødrene kom til prinsessen, kunne de ikke svare.
grammarPoints.explanation: Da means "when" but only for a single completed event in the past. Når is used instead for repeated past events, or present/future "when".

grammarPoints.rule: V2 inversion after a subordinate clause
grammarPoints.exampleSentence: Da brødrene kom til prinsessen, kunne de ikke svare.
grammarPoints.explanation: When a sentence opens with a subordinate clause, the main clause verb must come immediately after the comma, before its own subject — Danish main clauses always keep the verb in second position overall.

grammarPoints.exampleSentence: De kunne ikke svare på hendes spørgsmål.
grammarPoints.explanation: Negation with ikke comes right after the modal verb (kunne), not after the main verb (svare), which stays in its infinitive form at the end.
Sit/sin/sine refer back to the subject of the sentence ("his own word"). Hans would mean the word belonged to a different male person mentioned elsewhere.
